terms.definition: An inventory system in which suppliers deliver parts and materials exactly when production needs them, reducing storage costs and waste.
terms.trap: It does not mean keeping large backup inventories.
terms.example: A motorcycle plant receives seats and handlebars the same day they are installed.
terms.misconception: Just-in-Time Inventory means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: A production approach focused on eliminating waste, improving flow, and continuously improving processes.
terms.trap: Lean does not simply mean cutting jobs.
terms.example: A factory redesigns workstations to reduce wasted movement and defects.
terms.misconception: Lean Manufacturing means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: Producing large quantities of standardized goods at low cost.
terms.trap: It is not focused on individual customization.
terms.example: A factory produces thousands of identical pens.
terms.misconception: Mass Production means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: Producing goods tailored to individual customers while maintaining many efficiencies of mass production.
terms.trap: It is not one-at-a-time handmade production.
terms.example: A shoe company lets customers choose colors while using automated production.
terms.misconception: Mass Customization means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: Value added by changing raw materials or components into finished goods.
terms.trap: Do not confuse it with place or possession utility.
terms.example: Turning wheat into bread creates form utility.
terms.misconception: Form Utility means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: A facility layout where the product stays in one place while workers, tools, and materials come to it.
terms.trap: This is not the same as an assembly line.
terms.example: Ships and airplanes are often built using a fixed-position layout.
terms.misconception: Fixed-Position Layout means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: A layout where similar activities or equipment are grouped together by function.
terms.trap: It is not arranged around a single product flow.
terms.example: A hospital groups radiology, surgery, and lab services by process.
terms.misconception: Process Layout means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: A layout arranged around the sequence of steps needed to produce a specific product.
terms.trap: It is less flexible than a process layout.
terms.example: An automobile assembly line uses product layout.
terms.misconception: Product Layout means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: Using computer systems to create, modify, analyze, or optimize product designs.
terms.trap: CAD designs products. CAM helps manufacture them.
terms.example: An engineer uses software to design a machine part.
terms.misconception: Computer-Aided Design (CAD) means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: Using computers directly in the production process.
terms.trap: CAM is production-focused, not design-focused.
terms.example: A computer-controlled machine cuts metal parts.
terms.misconception: Computer-Aided Manufacturing (CAM) means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: A production system where computers control and integrate the entire manufacturing process.
terms.trap: It is broader than CAD or CAM alone.
terms.example: A car plant uses software to coordinate ordering, machines, and assembly.
terms.misconception: Computer-Integrated Manufacturing (CIM) means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: A manufacturing process that builds items layer by layer, often used for prototypes and molds.
terms.trap: It is not yet the main method for most mass-produced items.
terms.example: A company prints a prototype part before investing in tooling.
terms.misconception: 3D Printing means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: The maximum amount a firm can produce under normal conditions.
terms.trap: When demand exceeds capacity, firms may use overtime, extra shifts, or subcontracting.
terms.example: A bakery can produce 2,000 loaves per day.
terms.misconception: Capacity means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: Hiring another company to complete part of the work.
terms.trap: It is not the same as hiring full-time internal employees.
terms.example: A manufacturer hires another plant to make parts during peak demand.
terms.misconception: Subcontracting means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: Creating goods and services using land, labor, capital, entrepreneurship, and knowledge.
terms.trap: Production includes services, not just physical goods.
terms.example: A restaurant produces meals and service experiences.
terms.misconception: Production means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: Use of programmable machines to perform tasks in production or service operations.
terms.trap: Robots can raise productivity while reducing some labor needs.
terms.example: Robots weld car frames on an assembly line.
terms.misconception: Robotics means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: Buying products or materials from another country and bringing them into one’s own country.
terms.trap: Imports come into the country.
terms.example: A U.S. retailer buys clothing made in Vietnam.
terms.module: M02: Ethics, Social Responsibility & Global Business
terms.misconception: Importing means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: Selling products made in one country to buyers in another country.
terms.trap: Exports leave the country.
terms.example: A St. Louis company sells products to Germany.
terms.module: M02: Ethics, Social Responsibility & Global Business
terms.misconception: Exporting means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: The total value of a nation’s exports compared to its imports over a specific period.
terms.trap: Do not confuse it with balance of payments, which is broader.
terms.example: If exports are $1 trillion and imports are $1.2 trillion, the country has a trade deficit.
terms.module: M02: Ethics, Social Responsibility & Global Business
terms.misconception: Balance of Trade means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: An unfavorable balance of trade in which imports exceed exports.
terms.trap: A deficit means buying more from abroad than selling abroad.
terms.example: The United States has often imported more goods than it exports.
terms.module: M02: Ethics, Social Responsibility & Global Business
terms.misconception: Trade Deficit means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: A favorable balance of trade in which exports exceed imports.
terms.trap: A surplus does not automatically mean every worker benefits.
terms.example: A nation sells more goods abroad than it buys.
terms.module: M02: Ethics, Social Responsibility & Global Business
terms.misconception: Trade Surplus means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: The ability of a country to produce a good or service more efficiently relative to other goods it could produce.
terms.trap: Every country has some comparative advantage.
terms.example: A country focuses on products it makes most efficiently and trades for others.
terms.module: M02: Ethics, Social Responsibility & Global Business
terms.misconception: Comparative Advantage means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: The ability to produce more of a good using the same or fewer resources than another producer.
terms.trap: Comparative advantage matters more for trade decisions.
terms.example: One country can produce more wheat per acre than another.
terms.module: M02: Ethics, Social Responsibility & Global Business
terms.misconception: Absolute Advantage means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: Hiring workers in another country to perform work that could be done domestically.
terms.trap: Offshore outsourcing is not the same as importing a finished product from a supplier.
terms.example: A U.S. firm contracts customer service work to another country.
terms.module: M02: Ethics, Social Responsibility & Global Business
terms.misconception: Offshore Outsourcing means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: Foreign companies creating jobs domestically by locating operations in another country.
terms.trap: Insourcing can offset some jobs lost through outsourcing.
terms.example: A foreign automaker builds cars in Indiana or Ohio.
terms.module: M02: Ethics, Social Responsibility & Global Business
terms.misconception: Insourcing means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: A government limit on the quantity of a product that may be imported.
terms.trap: A quota limits quantity, while a tariff adds tax.
terms.example: A country allows only 100 million tons of sugar imports.
terms.module: M02: Ethics, Social Responsibility & Global Business
terms.misconception: Import Quota means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: A tax placed on imported goods.
terms.trap: Tariffs usually raise prices for consumers.
terms.example: A tariff adds cost to imported steel.
terms.module: M02: Ethics, Social Responsibility & Global Business
terms.misconception: Tariff means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: A complete ban on trade with a particular country or product.
terms.trap: An embargo is stronger than a quota or tariff.
terms.example: A country bans trade with a hostile nation.
terms.module: M02: Ethics, Social Responsibility & Global Business
terms.misconception: Embargo means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: A contractual agreement allowing one firm to produce and market another firm’s product or brand in exchange for a fee.
terms.trap: Licensing is often lower cost for the licensor, but control may be limited.
terms.example: A U.S. company permits a foreign firm to make its branded product.
terms.module: M02: Ethics, Social Responsibility & Global Business
terms.misconception: Licensing means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: A partnership between companies, often from different countries, to share resources, risks, and expertise.
terms.trap: It is not guaranteed profit.
terms.example: Two firms share marketing and management expertise in a foreign market.
terms.module: M02: Ethics, Social Responsibility & Global Business
terms.misconception: Joint Venture means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: A company with significant investments and physical operations in multiple countries.
terms.trap: Simply exporting does not make a company multinational.
terms.example: A company manufactures and markets products in several countries.
terms.module: M02: Ethics, Social Responsibility & Global Business
terms.misconception: Multinational Corporation means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: A government-controlled investment fund that invests in foreign companies and assets.
terms.trap: It is not a private mutual fund.
terms.example: A nation invests oil revenue in global companies.
terms.module: M02: Ethics, Social Responsibility & Global Business
terms.misconception: Sovereign Wealth Fund means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: A trade agreement among the United States, Mexico, and Canada that replaced NAFTA.
terms.trap: The member countries are not Panama or Nicaragua.
terms.example: Trade rules for North American goods are shaped by USMCA.
terms.module: M02: Ethics, Social Responsibility & Global Business
terms.misconception: USMCA means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: Trade among nations with reduced barriers such as tariffs and quotas.
terms.trap: Free trade can benefit consumers but may disrupt some workers and industries.
terms.example: Countries specialize and exchange goods more efficiently.
terms.module: M02: Ethics, Social Responsibility & Global Business
terms.misconception: Free Trade means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: The study of how society uses limited resources to produce and distribute goods and services.
terms.trap: Economics is about scarcity and choices.
terms.example: A country decides how to allocate labor, land, and capital.
terms.misconception: Economics means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: The study of an economy as a whole, including growth, inflation, unemployment, and GDP.
terms.trap: Macro is the big picture.
terms.example: Studying national economic growth is macroeconomics.
terms.misconception: Macroeconomics means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: The study of individual markets, firms, consumers, and pricing decisions.
terms.trap: Micro focuses on smaller units of the economy.
terms.example: Studying how one company sets prices is microeconomics.
terms.misconception: Microeconomics means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: The total dollar value of all final goods and services produced within a country’s borders during a specific period.
terms.trap: GDP measures production within borders, not just by citizens.
terms.example: Cars produced in Indiana count toward U.S. GDP.
terms.misconception: Gross Domestic Product (GDP) means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: A period commonly described as two consecutive quarters of decline in real GDP.
terms.trap: Recession is not the same as depression.
terms.example: Six months of shrinking economic output may indicate recession.
terms.misconception: Recession means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: An economic system in which individuals own and operate most businesses.
terms.trap: Capitalism relies on private ownership, competition, and profit motive.
terms.example: Entrepreneurs start firms to meet customer needs and earn profit.
terms.misconception: Capitalism means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: Adam Smith’s idea that people pursuing self-interest can unintentionally benefit society through jobs, goods, and wealth creation.
terms.trap: It does not mean greed is always good.
terms.example: A baker earns profit by selling bread customers want.
terms.misconception: Invisible Hand means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: Resources used to create wealth: land, labor, capital, entrepreneurship, and knowledge.
terms.trap: Capital in this context means tools, machinery, buildings, and technology, not just money.
terms.example: A factory uses land, workers, equipment, ideas, and risk-taking.
terms.misconception: Factors of Production means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: The quantity of a good or service producers are willing to sell at various prices.
terms.trap: Supply comes from producers.
terms.example: More sellers may supply coffee when prices rise.
terms.misconception: Supply means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: The quantity of a good or service consumers are willing and able to buy at various prices.
terms.trap: Wanting something is not demand unless buyers can pay.
terms.example: Students demand laptops when they need them and can afford them.
terms.misconception: Demand means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: The price at which quantity demanded equals quantity supplied.
terms.trap: Also called equilibrium price.
terms.example: A product sells at the price where buyers and sellers agree.
terms.misconception: Market Price means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: Recurring periods of economic growth and contraction.
terms.trap: Economies rarely grow at a perfectly steady pace.
terms.example: Expansion, peak, contraction, trough, recovery.
terms.misconception: Business Cycle means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: A market dominated by one seller.
terms.trap: A true monopoly has little or no direct competition.
terms.example: A local utility may operate as a regulated monopoly.
terms.misconception: Monopoly means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: A market dominated by a few large sellers.
terms.trap: A few dominant firms does not equal one monopoly.
terms.example: Commercial aircraft manufacturing is close to an oligopoly.
terms.misconception: Oligopoly means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: A market with many sellers offering similar but differentiated products.
terms.trap: Products are similar, not identical.
terms.example: A city with many bakeries selling different specialties.
terms.misconception: Monopolistic Competition means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: A market with many buyers and sellers offering nearly identical products.
terms.trap: Few real markets are perfectly competitive.
terms.example: Some agricultural commodity markets come close.
terms.misconception: Perfect Competition means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: Money left after a business subtracts expenses from revenue.
terms.trap: Revenue is not profit.
terms.example: A business earns $100,000 revenue and has $80,000 expenses, leaving $20,000 profit.
terms.misconception: Profit means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: When costs and expenses exceed revenues.
terms.trap: A loss means the business spent more than it earned.
terms.example: A startup spends $50,000 but earns $30,000.
terms.misconception: Loss means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: The chance of losing time, money, or resources invested in a business.
terms.trap: Entrepreneurs usually take calculated risks, not reckless risks.
terms.example: A new restaurant may fail if sales are too low.
terms.misconception: Risk means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: Efforts to increase available resources and improve how resources are used.
terms.trap: It is not just finding natural resources.
terms.example: Training workers improves human capital.
terms.misconception: Resource Development means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: Accepting the risk of starting and running a business.
terms.trap: Entrepreneurship is not just having an idea.
terms.example: A student launches a tutoring business and manages expenses, customers, and risk.
terms.misconception: Entrepreneurship means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: A person who organizes, manages, and assumes the risk of a business.
terms.trap: Entrepreneurs are usually action-oriented and tolerate uncertainty.
terms.example: Someone opens a repair shop after identifying local demand.
terms.misconception: Entrepreneur means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: An employee who uses company resources to develop new products, processes, or ventures.
terms.trap: Intrapreneurs innovate inside an existing organization.
terms.example: An employee develops a new service line for an employer.
terms.misconception: Intrapreneur means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: An independently owned and operated for-profit business that is not dominant in its field.
terms.trap: Small businesses can still have major local impact.
terms.example: A local HVAC company serving one region.
terms.misconception: Small Business means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: A business owned and usually managed by one person.
terms.trap: It is simple but carries unlimited liability.
terms.example: A freelance landscaper operates as a sole proprietor.
terms.misconception: Sole Proprietorship means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: A business owned by two or more people.
terms.trap: General partners may face unlimited liability.
terms.example: Two accountants start a firm together.
terms.misconception: Partnership means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: A partnership with at least one general partner and one or more limited partners.
terms.trap: Limited partners usually do not manage the business.
terms.example: An investor contributes money but does not run daily operations.
terms.misconception: Limited Partnership means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: A partner whose liability is limited to the amount invested and who does not manage the business.
terms.trap: Management activity can risk limited liability protection.
terms.example: A person invests $20,000 in a partnership and risks only that amount.
terms.misconception: Limited Partner means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: A partnership form that limits each partner’s personal liability for the acts of other partners.
terms.trap: Partners may still be liable for their own mistakes.
terms.example: Professional firms often use LLP structures.
terms.misconception: Limited Liability Partnership (LLP) means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: A business that acts like a corporation and is traded publicly but is taxed like a partnership.
terms.trap: It combines public trading with pass-through taxation.
terms.example: Some energy infrastructure companies use this form.
terms.misconception: Master Limited Partnership means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: A legal entity separate from its owners that can own property, enter contracts, and raise capital through stock.
terms.trap: Corporations usually involve more regulation and recordkeeping.
terms.example: A company sells shares to raise money for expansion.
terms.misconception: Corporation means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: A corporation whose stock is owned by a small group and not sold to the public.
terms.trap: Closed does not mean out of business.
terms.example: A family owns all shares of a corporation.
terms.misconception: Closed Corporation means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: A corporation in the state where it is incorporated.
terms.trap: The same corporation is foreign in other states where it operates.
terms.example: An Indiana-incorporated firm is domestic in Indiana.
terms.misconception: Domestic Corporation means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: A corporation doing business in a state other than the one where it is incorporated.
terms.trap: Foreign can mean another state, not necessarily another country.
terms.example: A Delaware corporation operating in Indiana is foreign in Indiana.
terms.misconception: Foreign Corporation means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: The top governing body of a corporation elected by shareholders.
terms.trap: The board oversees major decisions; officers manage daily operations.
terms.example: The board hires or evaluates the CEO.
terms.misconception: Board of Directors means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: The owner is personally responsible for all business debts and damages.
terms.trap: Personal assets may be at risk.
terms.example: A sole proprietor may have to use personal savings to pay business debts.
terms.misconception: Unlimited Liability means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: Owners’ losses are generally limited to the amount they invested.
terms.trap: Fraud or poor separation of business and personal finances can threaten protection.
terms.example: A shareholder usually risks only the money invested in stock.
terms.misconception: Limited Liability means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: A business arrangement where one party buys the right to use another company’s name, products, and business model.
terms.trap: Franchises reduce some risk but do not eliminate it.
terms.example: A local owner operates a fast-food restaurant under a national brand.
terms.misconception: Franchise means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: The company that grants the franchise rights.
terms.trap: The franchisor is the parent brand.
terms.example: The national pet store company grants rights to local owners.
terms.misconception: Franchisor means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: The person or business that buys the right to operate under the franchisor’s brand.
terms.trap: The franchisee follows brand rules and pays fees.
terms.example: A local owner operates a branded restaurant.
terms.misconception: Franchisee means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: A benefit of franchising, such as brand recognition, training, and a proven business model.
terms.trap: Brand recognition is a franchisee advantage; faster expansion and cash flow are franchisor advantages.
terms.example: A new owner benefits from customers already knowing the brand.
terms.misconception: Franchise Advantage means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: A drawback of franchising, such as high cost, strict rules, or risk of brand damage from poor locations.
terms.trap: A bad franchise location can hurt the franchisor’s reputation.
terms.example: Food safety problems at one location can damage the whole brand.
terms.misconception: Franchise Disadvantage means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: The process of using organizational resources to accomplish goals.
terms.trap: Management is not just giving orders.
terms.example: A manager coordinates people, budgets, and schedules.
terms.misconception: Management means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: Setting organizational goals and deciding how to achieve them.
terms.trap: Planning happens before organizing, leading, and controlling.
terms.example: A company decides to enter a new market.
terms.misconception: Planning means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: Designing the structure of the organization and arranging resources to meet goals.
terms.trap: Modern organizing often designs around customer needs.
terms.example: A company creates teams to serve specific customer segments.
terms.misconception: Organizing means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: Guiding, training, coaching, and motivating employees.
terms.trap: Leading is people-focused.
terms.example: A supervisor coaches employees through a service problem.
terms.misconception: Leading means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: Measuring progress, comparing results to standards, rewarding performance, and correcting problems.
terms.trap: Control is not only punishment.
terms.example: A manager reviews weekly sales and adjusts staffing.
terms.misconception: Controlling means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: A long-term plan that sets broad organizational direction.
terms.trap: Strategic plans address major long-term commitments.
terms.example: A firm decides whether to expand into new markets.
terms.misconception: Strategic Plan means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: A shorter-term plan developed by middle managers to implement strategic goals.
terms.trap: Tactical plans translate strategy into action.
terms.example: A department plans a campaign to support expansion.
terms.misconception: Tactical Plan means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: A detailed short-term plan for daily or weekly activities.
terms.trap: Operational plans are used closest to front-line work.
terms.example: A department manager creates weekly work assignments.
terms.misconception: Operational Plan means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: Preparing alternative plans in case conditions change.
terms.trap: It is the Plan B mindset.
terms.example: An ice cream business plans for bad weather during outdoor events.
terms.misconception: Contingency Planning means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: A statement explaining an organization’s purpose, values, philosophy, and self-concept.
terms.trap: A mission statement is broader than a slogan.
terms.example: A college states its commitment to student success.
terms.misconception: Mission Statement means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: A problem-solving method that encourages many ideas quickly without immediate criticism.
terms.trap: The first stage is idea generation, not judging.
terms.example: A team lists possible ways to improve customer service.
terms.misconception: Brainstorming means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: Senior executives responsible for overall direction and strategic decisions.
terms.trap: Top managers focus on vision and strategy.
terms.example: CEO, president, and executive vice presidents.
terms.misconception: Top Management means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: Managers who develop tactical plans and coordinate departments.
terms.trap: Middle managers connect top strategy to front-line work.
terms.example: Plant managers, deans, and division managers.
terms.misconception: Middle Management means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: First-line managers responsible for assigning specific jobs and evaluating daily worker performance.
terms.trap: Supervisors are closest to front-line employees.
terms.example: A shift supervisor assigns workstations.
terms.misconception: Supervisory Management means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: The ability to see the organization as a whole and understand how parts fit together.
terms.trap: Conceptual skills are especially important for top managers.
terms.example: A manager sees how marketing, operations, finance, and HR affect each other.
terms.misconception: Conceptual Skills means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: The ability to perform specific tasks or use specialized knowledge.
terms.trap: Technical skill is not the same as people skill.
terms.example: A warehouse manager understands inventory software.
terms.misconception: Technical Skills means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: The ability to work well with people.
terms.trap: Managers need interpersonal skills at every level.
terms.example: A supervisor resolves team conflict respectfully.
terms.misconception: Interpersonal Skills means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: A leadership style where the leader makes decisions and tells employees what to do.
terms.trap: Autocratic leadership can be fast but may limit employee input.
terms.example: A crisis response leader gives direct orders.
terms.misconception: Autocratic Leadership means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: A leadership style where leaders involve employees in decision-making.
terms.trap: It may take longer but can build commitment.
terms.example: A manager asks the team to help choose a process improvement.
terms.misconception: Democratic Leadership means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: A leadership style giving employees significant freedom in how they work.
terms.trap: It works best with skilled and self-directed employees.
terms.example: An experienced design team manages its own process.
terms.misconception: Laissez-Faire Leadership means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: Giving employees authority and responsibility to respond quickly to customer needs.
terms.trap: Empowerment requires trust and training.
terms.example: A hotel employee can resolve a guest problem without manager approval.
terms.misconception: Empowerment means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: The number of employees a manager supervises.
terms.trap: Wide spans are associated with flatter organizations.
terms.example: A supervisor with 15 direct reports has a wider span than one with 4.
terms.misconception: Span of Control means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: The line of authority moving from the top of the organization to the lowest level.
terms.trap: It clarifies who reports to whom.
terms.example: Employee to supervisor to manager to director.
terms.misconception: Chain of Command means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: An organization with fewer layers of management and wider spans of control.
terms.trap: Flat structures can respond faster to customers.
terms.example: A startup with few management levels.
terms.misconception: Flat Organization means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: An organization with many management layers and narrower spans of control.
terms.trap: Tall structures may slow decision-making.
terms.example: A large bureaucracy with many approval levels.
terms.misconception: Tall Organization means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: Dividing an organization into specialized units such as marketing, HR, accounting, and production.
terms.trap: Functional departmentalization groups by business function.
terms.example: A furniture company has separate marketing and production departments.
terms.misconception: Departmentalization means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: The oldest and simplest structure, with authority moving directly from person to person.
terms.trap: Line structure is straightforward but can be rigid.
terms.example: Owner to manager to worker.
terms.misconception: Line Organization means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: An organization with many layers, clear rules, procedures, and formal authority.
terms.trap: Weber favored rules and qualifications, but bureaucracy can become slow.
terms.example: A government agency with formal procedures.
terms.misconception: Bureaucratic Organization means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: Creating, communicating, delivering, and exchanging offerings that have value for customers, partners, and society.
terms.trap: Marketing is broader than advertising or selling.
terms.example: A company researches customers, designs a product, prices it, distributes it, and promotes it.
terms.misconception: Marketing means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: A philosophy based on customer orientation, service orientation, and profit orientation.
terms.trap: It focuses on learning about customers and exceeding expectations.
terms.example: A business adapts services based on customer needs.
terms.misconception: Marketing Concept means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: The combination of product, price, place/distribution, and promotion.
terms.trap: The four Ps are not the whole marketing process, but they are the core tools.
terms.example: A company chooses product features, price, stores, and ads.
terms.misconception: Marketing Mix means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: A good, service, or idea offered to satisfy customer needs.
terms.trap: A product includes benefits, image, service, and experience.
terms.example: A smartphone includes hardware, software, brand, warranty, and support.
terms.misconception: Product means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: The amount customers pay for a product.
terms.trap: Price affects customer perception and demand.
terms.example: A company chooses a premium or budget price.
terms.misconception: Price means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: The distribution decisions that make products available to customers.
terms.trap: Place is not just physical location.
terms.example: A product sold online and in retail stores.
terms.misconception: Place means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: Techniques sellers use to inform, remind, and persuade customers.
terms.trap: Promotion includes more than advertising.
terms.example: Salespeople, ads, social media, public relations, and coupons.
terms.misconception: Promotion means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: A specific group of customers toward which a firm directs its marketing efforts.
terms.trap: A target market is not everyone.
terms.example: A tutoring service targets first-year college students.
terms.misconception: Target Market means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: Individuals who want goods and services for personal use and have the resources to buy them.
terms.trap: Consumer markets are different from business-to-business markets.
terms.example: Households buying groceries.
terms.misconception: Consumer Market means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: Dividing the total market into groups with similar characteristics.
terms.trap: Segmentation helps firms target effectively.
terms.example: A fitness company targets beginners, athletes, and older adults differently.
terms.misconception: Market Segmentation means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: Gathering and analyzing information about customers, competitors, and markets to make marketing decisions.
terms.trap: Research should inform product, price, place, and promotion.
terms.example: A company surveys customers before launching a product.
terms.misconception: Marketing Research means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: Data gathered directly by the firm for a specific research purpose.
terms.trap: Surveys and interviews are primary data.
terms.example: A company interviews customers about product preferences.
terms.misconception: Primary Data means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: Existing data originally collected for another purpose.
terms.trap: Government reports and trade journals are secondary data.
terms.example: Using Census data to study demographics.
terms.misconception: Secondary Data means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: A small group discussion led by a moderator to gather opinions about products or issues.
terms.trap: Focus groups provide qualitative insight, not a large statistical sample.
terms.example: Ten customers discuss a new package design.
terms.misconception: Focus Group means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: Testing a product with potential users in a limited market before full launch.
terms.trap: It reduces risk but does not guarantee success.
terms.example: A restaurant tests a new menu item in one city.
terms.misconception: Test Marketing means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: Identifying external factors that can affect marketing success.
terms.trap: It includes social, competitive, economic, technological, and legal trends.
terms.example: A marketer tracks aging population trends.
terms.misconception: Environmental Scanning means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: A word, letter, or group of words or letters that distinguishes one seller’s goods or services.
terms.trap: The Nike swoosh is a trademark, not the brand name itself.
terms.example: Coca-Cola is a brand name.
terms.misconception: Brand Name means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: A legally protected brand symbol, design, or phrase.
terms.trap: A trademark protects identification in the marketplace.
terms.example: Nike’s swoosh is a trademark.
terms.misconception: Trademark means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: Creating real or perceived differences between products.
terms.trap: Differentiation helps avoid competing only on price.
terms.example: A bakery claims unique recipes and local ingredients.
terms.misconception: Product Differentiation means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: The total collection of products a company offers.
terms.trap: A product mix contains product lines.
terms.example: A company sells snacks, dairy, adhesives, and films.
terms.misconception: Product Mix means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: A group of related products within a company’s product mix.
terms.trap: Do not confuse product line with product mix.
terms.example: A cereal company’s breakfast cereals are one product line.
terms.misconception: Product Line means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: The stages a product goes through: introduction, growth, maturity, and decline.
terms.trap: The correct order matters.
terms.example: A new gadget launches, grows, levels off, and eventually declines.
terms.misconception: Product Life Cycle means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: A consumer product bought frequently with minimal effort.
terms.trap: Bread and gum are classic examples.
terms.example: Milk, snacks, and toothpaste.
terms.misconception: Convenience Product means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: A product customers compare on price, quality, and features before buying.
terms.trap: It requires more effort than a convenience product.
terms.example: Furniture or electronics.
terms.misconception: Shopping Product means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: A product customers strongly prefer and will make a special effort to buy.
terms.trap: Brand insistence can turn a product into a specialty good.
terms.example: A specific luxury watch brand.
terms.misconception: Specialty Product means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: A brand owned by a retailer or wholesaler.
terms.trap: Also called a store brand.
terms.example: A department store’s own jeans brand.
terms.misconception: Private Label Brand means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: A sequence of organizations that moves a product from producer to ultimate user.
terms.trap: Distribution channels may include wholesalers, retailers, and agents.
terms.example: Manufacturer to wholesaler to retailer to customer.
terms.misconception: Distribution Channel means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: An organization that helps move goods from producers to consumers.
terms.trap: Intermediaries can provide utility and efficiency.
terms.example: Wholesalers, retailers, and agents.
terms.misconception: Marketing Intermediary means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: An agent who represents several producers in a territory, as long as they do not represent competing products.
terms.trap: Agents do not usually take title to goods.
terms.example: A sales agent represents several equipment manufacturers.
terms.misconception: Manufacturer’s Agent means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: A wholesaler that performs all distribution functions.
terms.trap: They do more than store products.
terms.example: A wholesaler provides storage, delivery, credit, and sales support.
terms.misconception: Full-Service Wholesaler means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: A business that sells goods or services directly to final consumers.
terms.trap: Retailers are closest to the end consumer.
terms.example: A supermarket or discount store.
terms.misconception: Retailer means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: A large self-service retail store selling primarily food and household products.
terms.trap: A supermarket is not the same as a department store.
terms.example: Kroger or Meijer grocery sections.
terms.misconception: Supermarket means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: Charging a high initial price for a new product, then lowering it over time.
terms.trap: Skimming starts high.
terms.example: A new technology product launches at a premium price.
terms.misconception: Price Skimming means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: Charging a low initial price to attract customers and gain market share.
terms.trap: Penetration starts low.
terms.example: A streaming service offers a low introductory price.
terms.misconception: Penetration Pricing means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: The number of units that must be sold for total revenue to equal total cost.
terms.trap: At breakeven, profit is zero.
terms.example: A business must sell 500 units before covering all costs.
terms.misconception: Breakeven Quantity means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: Costs that do not change with the number of units produced in the short run.
terms.trap: Rent is usually fixed.
terms.example: Monthly lease payment.
terms.misconception: Fixed Costs means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: Costs that change based on the number of units produced.
terms.trap: Variable costs rise as production rises.
terms.example: Raw materials for each unit.
terms.misconception: Variable Costs means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: Value added by making products available where customers want them.
terms.trap: Shipping flowers from California to Indiana creates place utility.
terms.example: A store stocks snow shovels before winter.
terms.misconception: Place Utility means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: Value added by making products available when customers want them.
terms.trap: Time is about timing, not location.
terms.example: A convenience store stays open late.
terms.misconception: Time Utility means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: Value added by making it easier for customers to own or use a product.
terms.trap: Credit, delivery, and guarantees can create possession utility.
terms.example: A store offers financing for appliances.
terms.misconception: Possession Utility means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: Value added by providing helpful information to customers.
terms.trap: Knowledgeable salespeople and ads can provide information utility.
terms.example: A salesperson explains warranty options.
terms.misconception: Information Utility means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: Paying to have a product appear favorably in a television show, movie, or media content.
terms.trap: It is promotion disguised as part of entertainment.
terms.example: A character drinks a visibly branded soda.
terms.misconception: Product Placement means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: Advertising aimed at wholesalers and retailers to encourage them to carry a manufacturer’s products.
terms.trap: Trade advertising targets intermediaries, not final consumers.
terms.example: A manufacturer promotes products to grocery buyers.
terms.misconception: Trade Advertising means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: The steps salespeople follow to build relationships and complete sales.
terms.trap: Follow-up is the final step, not closing.
terms.example: A salesperson checks in after delivery to ensure satisfaction.
terms.misconception: Selling Process means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: Recording, classifying, summarizing, and interpreting financial information.
terms.trap: Accounting is more than bookkeeping.
terms.example: A business uses accounting to evaluate profitability.
terms.misconception: Accounting means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: The recording of business transactions.
terms.trap: Bookkeepers record; accountants interpret and report.
terms.example: Recording daily sales in a journal.
terms.misconception: Bookkeeping means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: The accounting record where transactions are first entered.
terms.trap: The journal comes before posting to ledgers.
terms.example: Recording a cash sale in the journal.
terms.misconception: Journal means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: A record that summarizes transactions by account.
terms.trap: The ledger organizes transactions after journal entries.
terms.example: All cash transactions are summarized in the cash ledger.
terms.misconception: Ledger means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: A summary used to verify that account ledgers are correct and balanced.
terms.trap: It is not one of the three major financial statements.
terms.example: Accountants prepare it before financial statements.
terms.misconception: Trial Balance means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: A financial statement showing assets, liabilities, and owners’ equity at a specific point in time.
terms.trap: It is a snapshot, not a period of activity.
terms.example: A balance sheet dated December 31.
terms.misconception: Balance Sheet means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: A financial statement showing revenue, expenses, and profit or loss over a period of time.
terms.trap: The income statement covers a period, unlike the balance sheet.
terms.example: A quarterly income statement shows profit for three months.
terms.misconception: Income Statement means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: A financial statement showing cash inflows and outflows from operating, investing, and financing activities.
terms.trap: Profit and cash flow are not always the same.
terms.example: A profitable firm may still have cash flow problems.
terms.misconception: Statement of Cash Flows means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: Resources owned by a business that have economic value.
terms.trap: Assets include cash, inventory, buildings, equipment, and patents.
terms.example: A delivery truck owned by the business.
terms.misconception: Assets means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: Debts or obligations owed by a business.
terms.trap: Liabilities reduce owners’ equity.
terms.example: A bank loan or unpaid supplier bill.
terms.misconception: Liabilities means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: Debts due within one year.
terms.trap: Current means short-term in accounting.
terms.example: Accounts payable due in 30 days.
terms.misconception: Current Liabilities means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: The owners’ claim on the business after liabilities are subtracted from assets.
terms.trap: Assets minus liabilities equals owners’ equity.
terms.example: If assets are $124,000 and liabilities are $31,000, equity is $93,000.
terms.misconception: Owners’ Equity means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: Nonphysical assets with value.
terms.trap: Patents and copyrights are intangible assets.
terms.example: A trademark or copyright.
terms.misconception: Intangible Assets means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: How quickly an asset can be converted into cash.
terms.trap: Cash is the most liquid asset.
terms.example: Inventory is less liquid than cash.
terms.misconception: Liquidity means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: A ratio measuring a firm’s ability to pay short-term debts.
terms.trap: It is a liquidity ratio.
terms.example: Current assets divided by current liabilities.
terms.misconception: Current Ratio means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: The business function responsible for acquiring and managing funds.
terms.trap: Finance focuses on funding decisions, not just recording transactions.
terms.example: Choosing whether to issue bonds or stock.
terms.misconception: Finance means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: Forecasting financial needs and planning how to obtain and use funds.
terms.trap: Planning looks forward.
terms.example: A business estimates cash needs for expansion.
terms.misconception: Financial Planning means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: Comparing actual revenues and expenses to projected amounts to identify variances.
terms.trap: Control compares plan to actual results.
terms.example: A manager compares actual payroll costs to the budget.
terms.misconception: Financial Control means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: A financial plan used to allocate resources and control spending.
terms.trap: A budget is not mainly for taxes.
terms.example: A department receives a monthly operating budget.
terms.misconception: Budget means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: A major investment in long-term assets such as buildings or equipment.
terms.trap: Capital expenditures often require large sums and long-term planning.
terms.example: Buying a building for office space.
terms.misconception: Capital Expenditure means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: Borrowed funds or credit expected to be repaid within one year.
terms.trap: Trade credit and commercial paper are short-term sources.
terms.example: A retailer receives 60 days to pay suppliers.
terms.misconception: Short-Term Financing means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: Borrowed funds or equity used for more than one year.
terms.trap: Corporate bonds are long-term debt financing.
terms.example: Issuing bonds to build a new facility.
terms.misconception: Long-Term Financing means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: A short-term financing arrangement where suppliers allow buyers to pay later.
terms.trap: It is common between manufacturers, wholesalers, and retailers.
terms.example: A retailer has 30 to 60 days to pay for merchandise.
terms.misconception: Trade Credit means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: Short-term unsecured debt issued by financially strong corporations.
terms.trap: It is not long-term financing.
terms.example: A corporation borrows short-term funds for operating needs.
terms.misconception: Commercial Paper means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: Selling accounts receivable to obtain cash quickly.
terms.trap: The firm gives up some value for faster cash.
terms.example: A business sells unpaid invoices to a finance company.
terms.misconception: Factoring means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: A loan backed by collateral.
terms.trap: Collateral lowers lender risk.
terms.example: A bank loan secured by equipment.
terms.misconception: Secured Loan means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: A debt security issued by a corporation or government to borrow money.
terms.trap: Bondholders are lenders, not owners.
terms.example: A city issues bonds to fund infrastructure.
terms.misconception: Bond means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: A long-term debt instrument issued by a corporation.
terms.trap: Interest paid on bonds is generally tax-deductible for the corporation.
terms.example: A corporation sells bonds to finance expansion.
terms.misconception: Corporate Bond means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: A fund established to ensure money is available to repay bondholders at maturity.
terms.trap: It is a repayment reserve.
terms.example: A company sets aside money annually to repay bonds.
terms.misconception: Sinking Fund means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: Raising funds by selling ownership in the company.
terms.trap: Common and preferred stock are forms of equity financing.
terms.example: A corporation sells shares to investors.
terms.misconception: Equity Financing means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: The basic form of corporate ownership, usually including voting rights and possible dividends.
terms.trap: Dividends are not guaranteed.
terms.example: Shareholders vote for the board of directors.
terms.misconception: Common Stock means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: Stock that gives owners preference in dividend payments before common stockholders.
terms.trap: Preferred stock often has limited or no voting rights.
terms.example: Preferred shareholders receive dividends first.
terms.misconception: Preferred Stock means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: Preferred stock where missed dividends must be paid before common stock dividends.
terms.trap: Cumulative means missed dividends accumulate.
terms.example: A company must pay skipped preferred dividends before common dividends.
terms.misconception: Cumulative Preferred Stock means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: After-tax profits distributed to stockholders.
terms.trap: Dividends are not expenses like bond interest.
terms.example: A corporation pays shareholders $1 per share.
terms.misconception: Dividends means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: After-tax profits kept in the business instead of distributed as dividends.
terms.trap: Retained earnings are an internal source of financing.
terms.example: A company reinvests profits into new equipment.
terms.misconception: Retained Earnings means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: The first sale of a corporation’s common stock to the general public.
terms.trap: An IPO makes shares publicly available for the first time.
terms.example: A private company becomes publicly traded.
terms.misconception: Initial Public Offering (IPO) means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: The federal agency that regulates securities markets and protects investors.
terms.trap: The SEC was created by the Securities Exchange Act of 1934.
terms.example: The SEC enforces disclosure rules for public companies.
terms.misconception: Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: Money that is quickly and easily accessed, including currency and checking deposits.
terms.trap: M-1 is the narrowest common measure.
terms.example: Cash in your wallet and checking account funds.
terms.misconception: Money Supply M-1 means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: M-1 plus additional near-money such as savings accounts, money market accounts, and some certificates of deposit.
terms.trap: M-2 includes everything in M-1 plus more.
terms.example: Savings account deposits are in M-2 but not M-1.
terms.misconception: Money Supply M-2 means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: An independent U.S. government agency that insures bank deposits to help maintain confidence in banks.
terms.trap: It was created to restore confidence in banking institutions.
terms.example: Depositors are protected up to legal insurance limits.
terms.misconception: FDIC means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: The central banking system of the United States responsible for monetary policy.
terms.trap: The system includes 12 Federal Reserve Banks.
terms.example: The Fed influences the money supply and interest rates.
terms.misconception: Federal Reserve System means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: Actions by the Federal Reserve to influence money supply and interest rates.
terms.trap: Fiscal policy is government taxing and spending.
terms.example: The Fed changes interest rate targets.
terms.misconception: Monetary Policy means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: A financial institution that accepts savings and checking deposits and provides home mortgage loans.
terms.trap: Historically focused on home mortgages.
terms.example: A local thrift provides mortgage loans.
terms.misconception: Savings and Loan Association means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: The value of one currency in terms of another.
terms.trap: When the dollar rises, foreign goods become cheaper for U.S. consumers.
terms.example: A stronger dollar lowers the dollar cost of imported goods.
terms.misconception: Exchange Rate means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: Activities involved in acquiring, developing, evaluating, compensating, and retaining employees.
terms.trap: HR is now important enough that many managers perform HR functions.
terms.example: A manager participates in interviewing, training, and evaluating employees.
terms.misconception: Human Resource Management means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: Determining the organization’s current and future staffing needs.
terms.trap: The first step is preparing a human resource inventory.
terms.example: A firm reviews current employee skills before forecasting hiring needs.
terms.misconception: Human Resource Planning means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: A list of current employees’ education, skills, training, experience, and career interests.
terms.trap: It helps identify internal talent.
terms.example: A company discovers which employees can move into supervisory roles.
terms.misconception: Human Resource Inventory means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: Activities used to attract the right number of qualified candidates.
terms.trap: Recruitment is about attracting candidates, selection is choosing among them.
terms.example: Posting jobs on Indeed or recruiting at colleges.
terms.misconception: Recruitment means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: Filling jobs from within the organization.
terms.trap: It can reduce cost and improve morale.
terms.example: Promoting a current employee.
terms.misconception: Internal Recruiting means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: Seeking job candidates from outside the organization.
terms.trap: Current employees are not an external source.
terms.example: Using employment agencies, college placement offices, or job websites.
terms.misconception: External Recruiting means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: A written statement explaining job duties, responsibilities, and working conditions.
terms.trap: Duties belong in the job description.
terms.example: A receptionist gives an applicant a document explaining daily responsibilities.
terms.misconception: Job Description means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: A written statement identifying minimum qualifications needed for a job.
terms.trap: Qualifications belong in the job specification.
terms.example: A job requires an associate degree and two years of experience.
terms.misconception: Job Specification means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: A program introducing new employees to the organization, job expectations, and policies.
terms.trap: Orientation is not the same as ongoing training.
terms.example: A new hire learns company rules and benefits.
terms.misconception: Orientation means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: Teaching employees skills needed to perform their current jobs.
terms.trap: Training is usually current-job focused.
terms.example: A worker learns how to operate a scanner.
terms.misconception: Training means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: Preparing employees for future responsibilities.
terms.trap: Development looks forward.
terms.example: A supervisor attends leadership development training.
terms.misconception: Development means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: Evaluating employee performance against standards.
terms.trap: Good appraisals provide feedback and guide improvement.
terms.example: An annual review evaluates goals and behavior.
terms.misconception: Performance Appraisal means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: Pay and benefits provided to employees.
terms.trap: Compensation includes more than wages.
terms.example: Salary, health insurance, retirement contributions, and bonuses.
terms.misconception: Compensation means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: A fixed amount of pay for a set period, often weekly, monthly, or annually.
terms.trap: Salary does not vary directly by hours worked in the same way hourly pay does.
terms.example: A supervisor earns $635 per week.
terms.misconception: Salary means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: A work schedule allowing employees to choose work hours within set limits.
terms.trap: Flextime still requires completing required hours.
terms.example: Employees work any 40 hours between 7 a.m. and 10 p.m.
terms.misconception: Flextime means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: Two part-time employees share one full-time job.
terms.trap: Not the same as flextime.
terms.example: Two workers split one receptionist role.
terms.misconception: Job Sharing means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: Building and maintaining professional relationships that can support career development.
terms.trap: Networking is not just asking for jobs.
terms.example: A student connects with employers after a job fair.
terms.misconception: Networking means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: The process of growing skills, responsibilities, and opportunities over time.
terms.trap: Career growth usually requires effort, not automatic promotion.
terms.example: A sales associate takes on more responsibility over several years.
terms.misconception: Career Development means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: Skills used to communicate clearly and credibly to an audience.
terms.trap: Slides should not be overloaded with jargon.
terms.example: A presenter uses keywords, visuals, eye contact, and rehearsal.
terms.misconception: Professional Presentation Skills means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: Part of the Civil Rights Act of 1964 prohibiting employment discrimination based on race, color, religion, sex, or national origin.
terms.trap: It applies to hiring, firing, training, compensation, and other employment matters.
terms.example: An employer cannot refuse to hire based on religion.
terms.misconception: Title VII means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: The Equal Employment Opportunity Commission, which investigates employment discrimination complaints and can sue employers.
terms.trap: EEOC is the correct agency, not a fictional employment office.
terms.example: A worker files a discrimination complaint with the EEOC.
terms.misconception: EEOC means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: Differences among people in the workforce.
terms.trap: Diversity is a workforce reality, not a passing business fad.
terms.example: Employees differ by background, age, language, experience, and culture.
terms.misconception: Cultural Diversity means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: Communication across cultures that requires clarity, respect, and avoidance of ambiguous expressions.
terms.trap: Humor can create misunderstandings and should be used carefully.
terms.example: Using short sentences in a global email.
terms.misconception: Intercultural Communication means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: Standards of right and wrong that guide behavior.
terms.trap: Legal behavior is not always ethical behavior.
terms.example: A decision may be legal but still unfair to customers.
terms.module: M02: Ethics, Social Responsibility & Global Business
terms.misconception: Ethics means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: Behavior that considers the effect of decisions on others and follows moral principles.
terms.trap: Ethics includes consequences for stakeholders.
terms.example: A manager refuses to mislead customers.
terms.module: M02: Ethics, Social Responsibility & Global Business
terms.misconception: Ethical Behavior means the same thing as any other concept in its category and does not require careful distinction.

terms.definition: The belief that ethical behavior depends on the situation and what seems best at the time.
terms.trap: It can weaken consistent standards.
terms.example: A person justifies dishonesty because it benefits them temporarily.
